title: Why the dad-bod is a different problem for desi men
summary: South Asian men have heart attacks an average of 6 years earlier than other ethnic groups, and 40 to 50% higher cardiovascular mortality in the UK.
The reason is body composition, not body weight. Visceral fat (around internal organs) and lower muscle mass drive the risk, and BMI underestimates it.
The fix is well-evidenced. Adequate protein, less refined carb, resistance training two or three times a week, and watching waist measurement rather than the scale.
description: Body composition, visceral fat, and why BMI underestimates heart risk for South Asian men.

title: The desi woman's guide to strength: 20s through 70s
summary: A meta-analysis of whey protein trials in women found average lean mass increase of just 0.37kg, less than 1% of body composition. The bulky myth is contradicted by every well-designed study.
What you build in your 20s matters for life. Each 0.5 SD increase in peak bone mass cuts lifetime fracture risk by around 40%.
Pregnancy adds 6.7 to 21.7g of protein needed per day. Lactation adds 19g. Most lacto-vegetarian Indian women fall well short of these targets.
description: Strength across the decades, peak bone mass, and protein needs from pregnancy to menopause.

title: Why ghee isn't the villain (but the rest of your meal might be)
summary: A 2024 meta-analysis of nearly 20,000 people found ghee consumption does not significantly affect overall lipid profiles in humans.
Ghee is more thermally stable than most seed oils. At cooking temperatures it produces dramatically fewer toxic compounds than sunflower or canola.
The bigger driver of desi cardiovascular risk is the refined carb load: biscuits, white rice, packet snacks, sweets. Not the ghee in the dal.
description: What the evidence says about ghee, seed oils, and the refined carb load in modern desi diets.

title: The truth about muscle loss after 40 (and why it hits desi adults harder)
summary: Adults lose 30 to 50% of their muscle mass between 40 and 80 if nothing is done about it. Most of that loss happens after 50.
South Asians start with less muscle than other ethnicities and face a steeper decline. In a UK study of 5,288 desi adults, low muscle strength was linked to 89% higher diabetes risk.
The fix is well-established: 1.0 to 1.2g of protein per kg of body weight per day, three meals each delivering 25 to 30g, plus regular resistance movement.
description: Sarcopenia after 40, why South Asians face a steeper curve, and what actually slows it.

title: Why your dal feels filling but you're hungry an hour later
summary: Feeling full and staying full are two different things. Desi meals nail the first, fall short on the second.
A typical desi lunch delivers 18 to 22g of protein. Your body needs 25 to 30g per meal to actually stay satisfied.
The fix isn't a different cuisine. It's a bigger portion of dal, more dahi, an egg, or a protein blend stirred in.
description: Why a full desi lunch leaves you hungry by 4pm — satiety, protein, and blood sugar.

title: Where does whey protein come from? The honest answer, with paneer.
summary: Whey is the yellow water left when milk splits into curds and liquid. Same thing your nani strains off when making paneer.
A litre of milk gives you 200g of paneer plus 800ml of whey, which holds around 5g of protein and most of the milk's vitamins.
Whey protein powder is just that water filtered and concentrated. Vegetarian. Not vegan.
description: Whey is the water left when you make paneer. Vegetarian, not vegan, and already in your kitchen.

title: Why protein matters more than you think: a guide for desi UK households
summary: NHS minimum is 0.75g/kg. Optimum for adults over 40 is 1.0 to 1.2g/kg.
A bowl of dal has 5 to 7g of protein, not the 18 to 22g most blogs claim.
Adults lose 30 to 50% of muscle mass between 40 and 80. Protein slows it.
description: How much protein desi UK households need, what dal really delivers, and the gap by decade.

title: High-protein tadka dal (nothing else about the pot changes)
summary: A proper main-course bowl of masoor dal carries about 12g of protein. One heaped tablespoon of Heldi stirred in at the table takes it past 22g.
The recipe is the classic: masoor, haldi, and a ghee tadka with jeera, garlic and tomato. Nothing about the pot changes.
Stir Heldi in once the bowl has stopped bubbling, or leave the jar on the table and let everyone dose their own.
description: The classic masoor dal with a ghee tadka, plus the one quiet edit that takes each bowl past 20g of protein.

recipe.name: High-protein tadka dal
recipe.keywords: tadka dal, high protein dal, masoor dal recipe, dal protein
recipeInstructions: Simmer the rinsed masoor with water, haldi and salt for 20 to 25 minutes until completely soft, skimming any foam.
Whisk the dal briefly to your preferred texture.
Heat the ghee, crackle the jeera, then cook the garlic until golden, the onion until soft, and the tomato with chilli powder until the ghee separates.
Pour the tadka over the dal, stir through and taste for salt.
Take the pot off the heat and let it settle until it has stopped bubbling.
Ladle into bowls and stir one heaped tablespoon of Heldi Khana into each until it disappears. Top with coriander.

title: High-protein cucumber raita (the quiet one on the table)
summary: A serving of cucumber raita has about 4g of protein. Whisk in a heaped tablespoon of Heldi and it climbs to about 15g.
No heat and no technique: whisk Heldi into the dahi first, then fold in the squeezed cucumber and roasted jeera.
It keeps in the fridge for a day, which makes it the easiest make-ahead protein move on the table.
description: Kheera raita with roasted jeera and kala namak, carrying 15g of protein per serving without changing how it tastes.

recipe.name: High-protein cucumber raita
recipe.keywords: cucumber raita, kheera raita, high protein raita, raita recipe
recipeIngredient: A pinch of Kashmiri chilli powder
recipeInstructions: Squeeze the grated cucumber until most of the water is out.
Whisk in the Heldi Khana one spoonful at a time, letting each disappear before the next.
Fold in the cucumber, roasted jeera powder and salt.
Top with chilli powder and herbs, then chill until dinner.
